title: Today's Tech Brief: AI costs how much? GitHub Copilot users react to new usage-based pricing system.
summary: GitHub Copilot's new usage-based pricing system has users reacting. What's behind the change and how does it impact users?
imageAlt: Image from source article for AI costs how much? GitHub Copilot users react to new usage-based pricing system.
imageCaption: AI costs how much? GitHub Copilot users react to new usage-based pricing system.
imageSourceTitle: AI costs how much? GitHub Copilot users react to new usage-based pricing system.

body.text: I'm Leo Stone, and welcome to Today's Tech Brief. I've been following GitHub's Copilot, an AI-powered coding assistant that's gained popularity among developers. Recently, GitHub announced a shift in its pricing model, moving from a flat rate to a usage-based system. This change has sparked reactions from users, and I'm here to break it down.

According to a report from Ars Technica, GitHub announced in April that it would be moving subscribers from a request-based billing system to a usage-based one. This means that users will now be charged based on the number of suggestions they use. For context, GitHub Copilot uses AI to predict and complete code lines, making it a valuable tool for developers. The change is interesting because it reflects the growing demand for AI-powered tools and the need for more flexible pricing models.

Another news item that caught my attention is the rise of AI-powered chatbots in customer service. Companies are increasingly using chatbots to handle customer inquiries, and it's amazing to see how far they've come. From simple automated responses to complex conversations, chatbots are changing the way we interact with businesses. I'm curious to see how this trend will evolve in the coming months.

My quick read is that today's set of stories is useful because it gives us texture, not a sermon. Each item has its own little job: one tells us what changed, another tells us what people are worried about, and another gives the day its mood.
So that is the brief version: a few headlines, a little context, and just enough connective tissue to make the day easier to follow without turning the whole thing into homework.
